---
title: "Plexus"
subtitle: "Toward a language and calculus of living systems"
---

Biology is not a single graph.

Living systems combine cells, molecules, tissues, populations, and continuous
fields across multiple spatial and temporal scales. Existing simulators
typically focus on one layer of this hierarchy at a time: particle mechanics,
cellular interactions, neural dynamics, reaction networks, or continuum fields.
As a result, models become difficult to combine, extend, and reason about.

Plexus starts from a different question:

> What is the minimal language needed to describe a living system?

Rather than building another domain-specific simulator, Plexus introduces a
common representation based on **hierarchical sets**, **continuous fields**, and
a small collection of **operators** acting between them. Cells contain particles
and metabolites. Populations contain cells. Fields exchange information with
discrete entities. Complex simulations emerge from composing a few primitive
operations.

The goal is not merely to predict trajectories, but to provide a *language* in
which biological systems can be expressed, modified, optimized, and understood.
A simulation becomes a program. A tissue becomes a composition of operators. A
new biological hypothesis becomes a small change to a specification rather than a
new software project.

## A language, not a world model

The goal of Plexus is not to learn a world model of biology. The goal is to
define a language in which biological worlds can be *written*.

Modern world models learn compressed latent representations whose internal
structure is optimized for prediction. Such representations can be powerful, but
their primitives are learned from data: they do not provide a natural language
for expressing biological hypotheses, composing mechanisms across scales, or
introducing new physical laws.

Plexus takes the opposite approach. Its primitives are **explicit**: sets,
fields, containment relations, and operators. A cell, a morphogen gradient, a
mechanical substrate, and a neural circuit are all represented within the same
formal framework. Dynamics arise from compositions of interpretable operators
rather than opaque latent transitions.

The question is therefore not *"Can we predict the next state?"* but *"What are
the primitive concepts required to describe a living system?"* In this view the
simulator is secondary — it is merely the **interpreter** of the language. The
central object is the representation itself. Once a living system is expressed in
the language of Plexus, it becomes possible to compose mechanisms across scales,
perform inverse inference, optimize biological designs, and generate new models
automatically.

Plexus proposes that hierarchical sets, fields, and **four classes of operators**
are sufficient to express a broad range of biological phenomena within a common
**differentiable** framework.

## Re-converging four frameworks

Plexus re-converges four sibling frameworks that all forked from
[`ParticleGraph`](https://github.com/allierc/ParticleGraph) and then drifted —
each implements *one* graph type; a tissue needs them coexisting.

| Framework | Domain | Becomes (in Plexus) |
|---|---|---|
| **CellGraph** | collective cell interactions | `Lateral` operators @ cell level |
| **NeuralGraph** | neural / signalling networks | `Lateral` operators @ cell level |
| **MPMGraph** | tissue mechanics, deformable matter | `Exchange` (P2G/G2P) @ particle level |
| **MetabolismGraph** | bipartite reaction networks | `Exchange` (stoichiometry) @ molecule level |

## What it looks like

Qualitatively different collective behaviours, all produced from the *same code*
by changing only a ~20-line specification — no new software per phenomenon.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
{{< video prototype/sim_1.mp4 title="aggregate — soft cells secrete a chemical and climb it" >}}

{{< video prototype/sim_3.mp4 title="swarm — strong boids make the cells flock and move as one" >}}

{{< video prototype/sim_6.mp4 title="sort — type-specific adhesion segregates two populations" >}}

{{< video prototype/ant_1.mp4 title="ant trails — stigmergic pheromone trails self-organize" >}}
:::

And because every simulation is differentiable, biological designs can be
*optimized*. Below, a foraging colony in a maze is tuned by black-box search —
from a hand-set design (left) to the best found (right), which delivers food
roughly five-fold faster.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
{{< video prototype/initial.mp4 title="initial hand-set colony" >}}

{{< video prototype/winner_7.mp4 title="optimized colony (winner 7)" >}}
:::

## Read the blueprint

This site distils a working prototype into a single blueprint, in four parts:

- **[The abstraction](abstraction.qmd)** — the missing primitive: a hierarchical
  graph container stated in the language of sets, fields, and four operators.
- **[LLM ↔ registry](methodology.qmd)** — the two-layer contract and the small
  declarative language that lets an LLM drive a registry of operators.
- **[Simulations](experiment.qmd)** — ten distinct simulations, an emergent
  ant-trail model, and a maze-foraging colony optimized end-to-end.
- **[Building Plexus](building.qmd)** — the proposed repository methodology and
  the invariants that hold the line.

A static version with full schematics and glossary is in
[`paper/plexus.pdf`](paper/plexus.pdf).
